sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Let me look at the segmentation more carefully. I see:
- Node 1: color=N (light green), pixels=128 - this is likely the green strip on the right edge
- Node 8: color=g, pixels=9 - center cell (gray)
- Node 9: color=W, pixels=9 - one of the white cross cells
- Nodes 13, 14: color=p (purple), pixels=4 each - purple dots in the left panel
So the grid has:
- Center: gray (g, 9 pixels = 3x3)
- Cross cells: white (W, 9 pixels each = 3x3)
- Corner cells: gray (g, 9 pixels each)
The left panel has purple dots (p, 4 pixels each = 2x2).
Now I need to understand the game mechanics better. Let me think about what the bar cursor does.
The bar has a cursor that can move LEFT/RIGHT and UP/DOWN. The cursor position seems to map to grid cell positions. When I click on a grid cell, the cursor position determines what color gets applied.
But I've only seen gray and green being applied. I need to find a way to apply purple or white.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the game. Maybe the bar cursor doesn't select colors. Maybe it selects which grid cell to toggle. The cursor has 2 vertical positions and multiple horizontal positions. If I map the cursor position to grid cells:
- Cursor row 1 (upper): grid row 0 or 1
- Cursor row 2 (lower): grid row 1 or 2
- Cursor col position: grid col 0, 1, or 2
So there are 2 * 3 = 6 or 2 * 9 = 18 possible cursor positions, mapping to 9 grid cells.
When I click on the bar (not the grid), it might toggle the corresponding grid cell.
Let me test this by clicking on the bar at different positions and seeing which grid cell toggles.
Actually, I realize I should try a completely different approach. Let me look at what happens when I click on the bar track itself (not the blue block, not the grid).

</parameter>
